content.problemStatement:
You are given a matrix with nnn rows and mmm columns containing only 000s and 111s.

Each row is sorted in ascending order.

Find the index of the row with the **maximum number of 111’s**.

If multiple rows have the same number of 111’s, choose the row with the smaller index.

If there is **no 1** in the matrix, return −1-1−1.

Function DeclarationFunction Name**findRowWithMaxOnesfindRowWithMaxOnesfindRowWithMaxOnes**

Parameters
matmatmat : A 2D matrix containing only 000s and 111s.
nnn : Number of rows in the matrix.
mmm : Number of columns in the matrix.
Return Value
Returns an integer — the **index of the row** with the maximum number of 111s.
Returns −1-1−1 if the matrix contains **no** 111s.
Constraints
1≤n,m≤1041 \leq n, m \leq 10^41≤n,m≤104
mat[i][j]∈0,1mat[i][j] ∈ {0, 1}mat[i][j]∈0,1
Each row is sorted in **ascending order**

Input Format

The first line contains two integers nnn and mmm — the number of rows and columns.
The next nnn lines each contain mmm integers (000 or 111) representing the matrix rows.



Output Format

Return a single integer — the index of the row with the maximum number of 111s.
Return −1-1−1 if no 111 exists in the matrix.
constraints: Each row is sorted in ascending order
content.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case:
- The first line contains two integers n and m — the number of rows and columns.
- The next n lines each contain m integers (0 or 1) separated by spaces, representing a row of the matrix. Each row is sorted in non‑decreasing order (all 0s come before 1s).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single integer on its own line — the 0‑based index of the row that contains the maximum number of 1s. If the matrix contains no 1 at all, output -1.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Row 3 (0‑based) contains five 1s, which is more than any other row.
Rows 1 and 2 each have exactly one 1; the smaller index (1) is chosen.
The only row (index 0) has a single 1.

solution.approach: Because every row is sorted (all 0s then 1s), the first 1 in a row marks the start of all 1s in that row. Starting from the top‑right corner (row 0, column m‑1) we can move only left when we see a 1 (meaning this row has more 1s) and move down when we see a 0 (meaning this row cannot have more 1s than rows below). While traversing we keep the best row index and the maximum number of 1s seen so far. The traversal visits at most n+m cells, giving O(n+m) time and O(1) extra space per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(n + m) per test case, where n is the number of rows and m the number of columns.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) extra space (apart from storing the input matrix).
